sourceQuestion:
Context-free languages and regular languages are both closed under the operation(s) of:
A. Union
B. Intersection
C. Concatenation
D. Complementation

parsed.stem: Context-free languages and regular languages are both closed under the operation(s) of:

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is true?
A. The complement of a recursive language is recursive
B. The complement of a recursively enumerable language is recursively enumerable
C. The complement of a recursive language is either recursive or recursively enumerable
D. The complement of a context-free language is context-free
sourceAnswer: A. The complement of a recursive language is recursive

parsed.stem: Which of the following is true?

options.text: The complement of a recursive language is recursive
The complement of a recursively enumerable language is recursively enumerable
The complement of a recursive language is either recursive or recursively enumerable
The complement of a context-free language is context-free

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is/are FALSE?
1. For every non-deterministic Turing machine, there exists an equivalent deterministic Turing machine.
2. Turing recognizable languages are closed under union and complementation.
3. Turing decidable languages are closed under intersection and complementation.
4. Turing recognizable languages are closed under union and intersection.
A. 2 and 4
B. 1 and 3
C. 2 only
D. 3 only

parsed.stem:
Which of the following statements is/are FALSE?
1. For every non-deterministic Turing machine, there exists an equivalent deterministic Turing machine.
2. Turing recognizable languages are closed under union and complementation.
3. Turing decidable languages are closed under intersection and complementation.
4. Turing recognizable languages are closed under union and intersection.

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following types of languages:
L_1: Regular, L_2: Context-free, L_3: Recursive, L_4: Recursively enumerable.
Which of the following statements is TRUE?
A. Only L_1 and L_2 are closed under complementation
B. Only L_1 and L_3 are closed under complementation
C. Only L_2 and L_4 are closed under complementation
D. All four are closed under complementation
sourceAnswer: B. Only L_1 and L_3 are closed under complementation

parsed.stem:
Consider the following types of languages:
L_1: Regular, L_2: Context-free, L_3: Recursive, L_4: Recursively enumerable.
Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.text: Only L_1 and L_2 are closed under complementation
Only L_1 and L_3 are closed under complementation
Only L_2 and L_4 are closed under complementation
All four are closed under complementation

sourceQuestion:
Let L_1 and L_2 be any two context-free languages and R be any regular language. Then which of the following is/are CORRECT?

I. L_1 union L_2 is context-free
II. L_1 intersect L_2 is context-free
III. L_1 - R is context-free
IV. R - L_1 is context-free

A. I, II and IV only
B. I and III only
C. II and IV only
D. I only

parsed.stem:
Let L_1 and L_2 be any two context-free languages and R be any regular language. Then which of the following is/are CORRECT?

I. L_1 union L_2 is context-free
II. L_1 intersect L_2 is context-free
III. L_1 - R is context-free
IV. R - L_1 is context-free

sourceQuestion:
The set of all recursively enumerable languages is:

A. closed under complementation
B. closed under intersection
C. a subset of the set of all recursive languages
D. an uncountable set

parsed.stem: The set of all recursively enumerable languages is:

options.text: a subset of the set of all recursive languages

sourceQuestion:
Consider the two lists List I and List II given below:

List I:
(i) Context-free languages
(ii) Recursive languages
(iii) Regular languages

List II:
(a) Closed under union
(b) Not closed under complementation
(c) Closed under intersection

For matching of items in List I with those in List II, which of the following option(s) is/are CORRECT?

A. (i) - (a), (ii) - (b), and (iii) - (c)
B. (i) - (b), (ii) - (a), and (iii) - (c)
C. (i) - (b), (ii) - (c), and (iii) - (a)
D. (i) - (a), (ii) - (c), and (iii) - (b)

parsed.stem:

List II:
(a) Closed under union
(b) Not closed under complementation
(c) Closed under intersection

For matching of items in List I with those in List II, which of the following option(s) is/are CORRECT?

sourceQuestion:
Let L be a context-free language and M be a regular language. Then the language L intersect M is:

A. always regular
B. never regular
C. always a deterministic context-free language
D. always a context-free language

parsed.stem: Let L be a context-free language and M be a regular language. Then the language L intersect M is:

sourceAnswer: A. L(G) = {a^m b | m >= 1} union {a^2 b^n | n >= 2}

parsed.stem:
Consider the following context-free grammar G, where S, A, and B are the variables (non-terminals), and a and b are the terminal symbols. S is the start variable, and the rules of G are described as:
S -> aaBB | Ab
A -> a | aA
B -> b | bB

Which ONE of the languages L(G) is accepted by G?

options.text: L(G) = {a^m b | m >= 1} union {a^2 b^n | n >= 2}
L(G) = {a^m b^n | m >= 1, n >= 2} union {a^2 b^n | n >= 1}
L(G) = {a^n b^n | n >= 1} union {a^2 b^n | n >= 2}

sourceQuestion:
Let G_1, G_2 be Context Free Grammars (CFGs) and R be a regular expression. For a grammar G, let L(G) denote the language generated by G. Which ONE among the following questions is decidable?

A. Is L(G_1) = L(G_2)?
B. Is L(G_1) ∩ L(G_2) = emptyset?
C. Is L(G_1) = L(R)?
D. Is L(G_1) = emptyset?

parsed.stem: Let G_1, G_2 be Context Free Grammars (CFGs) and R be a regular expression. For a grammar G, let L(G) denote the language generated by G. Which ONE among the following questions is decidable?

sourceQuestion:
Context-free languages are: (Select all that apply)

A. closed under union
B. closed under complementation
C. closed under intersection
D. closed under Kleene closure

parsed.stem: Context-free languages are: (Select all that apply)

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following definitions below generate the same language as L, where L = {x^n y^n | n >= 1}?
I.   E -> xEy | xy
II.  E -> xEy | epsilon
III. E -> x^i y^i such that i >= 1

A. I only
B. I and II
C. II and III
D. II only

parsed.stem:
Which of the following definitions below generate the same language as L, where L = {x^n y^n | n >= 1}?
I.   E -> xEy | xy
II.  E -> xEy | epsilon
III. E -> x^i y^i such that i >= 1

sourceQuestion:
If L1 and L2 are context free languages and R is a regular set, one of the languages below is not necessarily a context free language. Which one?
A. L1 . L2
B. L1 ∩ R
C. L1 ∩ L2
D. L1 U L2

parsed.stem: If L1 and L2 are context free languages and R is a regular set, one of the languages below is not necessarily a context free language. Which one?

sourceQuestion:
Define a context free language L_init(L) = {u | u is a prefix of some string in L} (in other words, init(L) is the set of all prefixes of strings in L).
Let L = {0^m 1^m | m >= 0}.
Then init(L) is:
A. the set of all binary strings with unequal number of 0's and 1's
B. the set of all binary strings including the null string
C. the set of all binary strings with exactly one more 1 than the number of 0's
D. None of the above

parsed.stem:
Define a context free language L_init(L) = {u | u is a prefix of some string in L} (in other words, init(L) is the set of all prefixes of strings in L).
Let L = {0^m 1^m | m >= 0}.
Then init(L) is:

options.text: the set of all binary strings with unequal number of 0's and 1's
the set of all binary strings including the null string
the set of all binary strings with exactly one more 1 than the number of 0's

sourceQuestion:
Context-free languages are closed under:
A. Union, intersection
B. Union, Kleene closure
C. Intersection, complement
D. Complement, Kleene closure

parsed.stem: Context-free languages are closed under:

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is true?
A. If a language is context free it can always be accepted by a deterministic push-down automaton
B. The union of two context free languages is context free
C. The intersection of two context free languages is context free
D. The complement of a context free language is context free
sourceAnswer: B. The union of two context free languages is context free

parsed.stem: Which of the following statements is true?

options.text: If a language is context free it can always be accepted by a deterministic push-down automaton
The union of two context free languages is context free
The intersection of two context free languages is context free
The complement of a context free language is context free

sourceQuestion:
Let G = ({S}, {a, b}, R, S) be a context free grammar where the rule set R is:
  S -> aSb | SS | epsilon
Which of the following statements is true?
A. G is not ambiguous
B. There exist strings in L(G) such that every derivation of that string has at most 2 steps
C. There is a deterministic pushdown automaton that accepts L(G)
D. We can find a deterministic finite state automaton that accepts L(G)
sourceAnswer: C. There is a deterministic pushdown automaton that accepts L(G)

parsed.stem:
Let G = ({S}, {a, b}, R, S) be a context free grammar where the rule set R is:
  S -> aSb | SS | epsilon
Which of the following statements is true?

options.text: There exist strings in L(G) such that every derivation of that string has at most 2 steps
There is a deterministic pushdown automaton that accepts L(G)
We can find a deterministic finite state automaton that accepts L(G)

sourceAnswer: B. L_2 is a deterministic CFL

options.text: L_3 is a CFL, but not a deterministic CFL

sourceQuestion:
Let L_2 = {0^(m+1) 1^n | n, m >= 0}, L_3 = {0^(m+1) 1^(m+n) | n, m >= 0} and L_4 = {0^(m+1) 1^(m+n) 2^m | n, m >= 0}. Which of these languages are NOT context free?
A. L_2 only
B. L_3 only
C. L_2 and L_3
D. L_3 and L_4

parsed.stem: Let L_2 = {0^(m+1) 1^n | n, m >= 0}, L_3 = {0^(m+1) 1^(m+n) | n, m >= 0} and L_4 = {0^(m+1) 1^(m+n) 2^m | n, m >= 0}. Which of these languages are NOT context free?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following grammar over the alphabet {a, b}:
S -> aSb | bSa | b
The language generated by the above grammar over the alphabet {a, b} is the set of:
A. all palindromes
B. all odd length palindromes
C. strings that begin and end with the same symbol
D. all even length palindromes

parsed.stem:
Consider the following grammar over the alphabet {a, b}:
S -> aSb | bSa | b
The language generated by the above grammar over the alphabet {a, b} is the set of:

options.text: strings that begin and end with the same symbol

parsed.stem:
Which of the following languages is generated by the given grammar?
S -> aS | bS | epsilon

options.text: {w | w has equal number of a's and b's}
{w | w has more a's than b's}

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following context-free grammars:
G_1: S -> aS | bS | b
G_2: S -> SaS | SbS | e (where e denotes epsilon)
Which one of the following pairs of languages is generated by G_1 and G_2, respectively?
A. {a^n b^m | n >= 0, m > 0} and {a, b}*
B. {a, b}+ and {a, b}*
C. {a^n b | n >= 0} and {a, b}*
D. {a^n b^m | n >= 0, m > 0} and {a^n b^n | n >= 0}
sourceAnswer: A. {a^n b^m | n >= 0, m > 0} and {a, b}*

parsed.stem:
Consider the following context-free grammars:
G_1: S -> aS | bS | b
G_2: S -> SaS | SbS | e (where e denotes epsilon)
Which one of the following pairs of languages is generated by G_1 and G_2, respectively?

options.text: {a^n b^m | n >= 0, m > 0} and {a^n b^n | n >= 0}

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following languages:
L1 = {a^m b^n | m > 0 or n > 0}
L2 = {a^m b^n | m > 0 and n > 0}

Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. Both L1 and L2 are context-free.
B. L1 is context-free while L2 is not context-free.
C. L2 is context-free while L1 is not context-free.
D. Neither L1 nor L2 is context-free.
sourceAnswer: A. Both L1 and L2 are context-free.

options.text: Both L1 and L2 are context-free.
L1 is context-free while L2 is not context-free.
L2 is context-free while L1 is not context-free.
Neither L1 nor L2 is context-free.

parsed.stem:
Consider the following context-free grammar over the alphabet {a, b, c} with S as the start symbol:
S -> abScT | abcT
T -> bT | b

Which one of the following represents the language generated by the above grammar?

sourceQuestion:
If G is a grammar with productions
S -> SaS | aSb | bSa | SS | epsilon
where S is the start variable, then which one of the following strings is NOT in L(G)?
A. abab
B. abba
C. aabb
D. bbaa

parsed.stem:
If G is a grammar with productions
S -> SaS | aSb | bSa | SS | epsilon
where S is the start variable, then which one of the following strings is NOT in L(G)?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following languages over the alphabet Sigma = {a, b, c}.
Let L1 = {a^n b^n c^m | m, n >= 0} and L2 = {a^m b^n c^n | m, n >= 0}.

Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. L1 only is context-free.
B. L2 only is context-free.
C. Both L1 and L2 are context-free.
D. Neither L1 nor L2 is context-free.
sourceAnswer: C. Both L1 and L2 are context-free.

parsed.stem:
Consider the following languages over the alphabet Sigma = {a, b, c}.
Let L1 = {a^n b^n c^m | m, n >= 0} and L2 = {a^m b^n c^n | m, n >= 0}.

Which one of the following is TRUE?

options.text: Both L1 and L2 are context-free.
Neither L1 nor L2 is context-free.

parsed.stem:
Identify the language generated by the following grammar, where S is the start variable.

S -> XY
X -> aX | a
Y -> aYb | epsilon

sourceAnswer: B. {wa^n b^n w^R | w in {a, b}^*, n >= 0}

parsed.stem: Which one of the following languages over Sigma = {a, b} is NOT context-free?

options.text: {wa^n b^n w^R | w in {a, b}^*, n >= 0}
{wa^n b^n w^R | w in {a, b}^*, n >= 0}
{a^i b^j c^k | i, j, k in {3n, 5n | n >= 0}}
